---
title: "The Engine Room"
type: primer
provenance: "synthesis"
---

*Silhouette: Under every game runs an engine, the rules that decide what happens when a player acts.*

<!-- Primer: orientation to the Systems & Mechanics family. Light authorial / Mentor voice. -->

## The Terrain

This family is about the engine under the game. Every other family works on something the player meets directly. The puzzle in front of them. The prop they pick up. The shape a story takes, or the people sitting across the table. This one works on the part nobody looks at while it runs. When a player acts and the game answers, something decided that answer. That something is the engine, and it is made of rules. A single die roll is one. A campaign-long clock is another. They sit at opposite ends of the same machinery. [synthesis]

The engine does three things at once, and most of the design space is just different ways of balancing them. First it turns choices into outcomes. A player tries something with the result in doubt, and the rules tell the table how the doubt resolves. Second it sets the feel of that uncertain moment. A flat die makes luck sharp and real. A bell curve insists competence usually shows. Take the randomiser away entirely and the drama was never in the variance at all. A resolution method is a claim about what an uncertain moment should feel like. That claim is locked in long before a word of setting text exists. Third and most quietly of all the engine makes an argument about what the game is for. [synthesis]

That last job is the one to hold on to, because it is the one that gets missed. A rule is never neutral. The number that tracks a character's sanity, the lock that opens only at trust 5, the bullet too precious to fire: each says something about its world whether the designer meant it or not. A worker-placement engine that reskins from Spanish colonisation to Norse myth without changing a rule was wearing its theme as paint. A game whose rules stop making sense the moment you strip the setting away meant every one of them. The board game makes this plainest, because it has no prose to fall back on, but it holds for a dice mechanic and a relationship meter just as hard. The mechanic is the message. [synthesis]

So the question this family keeps returning to is small and concrete. It runs unchanged from a lone d6 up to a faction's offscreen scheme. What does this rule make true, and what does it mean? Get the first half wrong and the engine stalls or grinds. Get the second half wrong and it runs beautifully while saying nothing, or worse, while saying something you never intended. Both halves have to be answered together. [synthesis]

::: {.callout-tip}
### Mentor Wisdom
Build the smallest engine that produces the behaviour you want, then stop. The instinct when a system feels thin is to add a subsystem, an exception, a second resource to track. Resist it. Depth is the behaviour a rule set throws off, not the rules it costs, and the two are easy to confuse because more rules feel like more game. Add a part only when you can name the behaviour it buys and confirm the cheaper parts already in the box do not buy it. A page of rules that keeps surprising the people who wrote it beats a tome that has an answer for everything and a pulse for nothing. [synthesis]
:::

## Key Mechanisms

**The resolution core.** The floor the whole family stands on is [Mechanical Resolution Types](../articles/systems-mechanics/mechanical-resolution-types.qmd), the roll at the centre of the table where the game finds out what happens next, and where the shape of the odds is already an argument about what an uncertain moment should mean. The sentence wrapped around that roll, the loop where fiction names the trigger and the dice answer back in fiction, is [Moves & the Fiction-Mechanic-Fiction Loop](../articles/systems-mechanics/moves-and-the-fiction-mechanic-fiction-loop.qmd), home of the canonical PbtA Move. Who acts when and how much they get to do is [Turn Structure & Action Economy](../articles/systems-mechanics/turn-structure-and-action-economy.qmd). The procedure a lone player consults to be told something they did not decide, the engine that stands in for an absent GM, is [Oracle & Solo-Play Resolution](../articles/systems-mechanics/oracle-and-solo-play-resolution.qmd). And the bounded scene with something genuinely at stake, the unit a session is built from, is [Encounter & Challenge Design](../articles/systems-mechanics/encounter-and-challenge-design.qmd).

**The traditions that frame it.** No engine is chosen in a vacuum. A roleplaying game belongs to one design family or another, and the map that sorts those families by where authority lives and how tightly the rules grip the fiction is [TTRPG Format Taxonomy](../articles/systems-mechanics/ttrpg-format-taxonomy.qmd). Whether a game's rules are its world spoken twice or a neutral engine the world is poured into is [System vs Setting](../articles/systems-mechanics/system-vs-setting.qmd).

**Character and state.** A character handed over whole, identity and rules in one packet playable before the first scene opens, is [Playbook Design](../articles/systems-mechanics/playbook-design.qmd). The circle that fills one wedge at a time so the table can see the world moving while the players look the other way is [Progress Clocks & Faction State](../articles/systems-mechanics/progress-clocks-and-faction-state.qmd), home of the canonical Progress Clock.

**The game-runner's economy.** The hours spent before play, where the trick is telling the prep that carries the table from the prep the players walk straight past, is [Prep Economy](../articles/systems-mechanics/prep-economy.qmd). Safety built into the rules themselves, so the pause fires without waiting on anyone's nerve to call it, is [Safety Systems as Mechanical Layer](../articles/systems-mechanics/safety-systems-as-mechanical-layer.qmd). And the rules that decide whose turn it is to say what is true, once the GM is no longer the only voice, are [Distributing Narrative Authority](../articles/systems-mechanics/distributing-narrative-authority.qmd).

**The boardgame design principles.** The moment a player weighs two real options and neither one is free is [Decision Space & Choice Design](../articles/systems-mechanics/decision-space-and-choice-design.qmd). The few rules that keep paying out, and the chance that keeps the table honest, are [Elegance, Emergence & Randomness](../articles/systems-mechanics/elegance-emergence-and-randomness.qmd). The test of whether a game's rules mean its setting or merely wear it, the family's clearest statement of mechanism-as-argument, is [Theme-Mechanism Integration](../articles/systems-mechanics/theme-mechanism-integration.qmd). Whether a game is still worth opening the second time and the tenth is [Replayability](../articles/systems-mechanics/replayability.qmd). The opponent in a box, simulated well enough to play against alone, is [Solo Modes](../articles/systems-mechanics/solo-modes.qmd).

**The boardgame genres.** Two genres built on a single shared fate, one across a table and one across a campaign that never resets, are [Cooperative & Legacy Genres](../articles/systems-mechanics/cooperative-and-legacy-genres.qmd). The genres where the rules are a thin frame and the players themselves are the thing being played are [Social Performance Genres](../articles/systems-mechanics/social-performance-genres.qmd).

**The digital and IF edge.** The rules that carry an argument the prose only describes, where a stat climbing half a point makes a claim as loud as a sentence, are [IF Mechanics & Procedural Rhetoric](../articles/systems-mechanics/if-mechanics-and-procedural-rhetoric.qmd). The software behind the curtain that decides whether an answer was right and lets the player through is [Digital Gating & State](../articles/systems-mechanics/digital-gating-and-state.qmd).

**A boardgame reference appendix.** Three articles form a tabletop-only reference cluster for boardgame designers, mapping the raw vocabulary the rest of the family draws on. The shared names for the decision-shapes that hobby boardgames are built from are in [Boardgame Mechanism Vocabulary](../articles/systems-mechanics/boardgame-mechanism-vocabulary.qmd). The two poles the hobby has argued about for thirty years, the clean machine against the lived story, are [Euro vs Ameritrash: The Two Design Poles](../articles/systems-mechanics/euro-vs-ameritrash-the-two-design-poles.qmd). And the genre where the deck you draw from is the thing you spend the whole game rebuilding is [Deckbuilders](../articles/systems-mechanics/deckbuilders.qmd).

## Related Exemplars

*Apocalypse World* (Vincent Baker, 2010) is the resolution method chosen as an argument rather than a default: its 2d6 curve puts the partial success on the fattest part of the bell so that things always get complicated by design. *Blades in the Dark* (John Harper, 2017) made the Progress Clock a household tool and built faction state into the engine, so the world moves whether the crew is watching or not. *Dominion* (Donald X. Vaccarino, 2008) founded the deckbuilder, where the deck stops being a thing you play from and becomes the thing you sculpt while playing. And *Spirit Island* (R. Eric Reuss, 2017) is mechanism-as-argument at its most pointed, an anti-colonial thesis welded into the rules tightly enough that the welding itself drew both praise and complaint.
